reasoning: The finding claims that JSON.parse(toUtf8(bytes)) at line 3883 of index.node.js causes prototype pollution when an attacker-controlled decrypted payload contains {"__proto__":{"isAdmin":true}}. This is factually incorrect. V8's native JSON.parse does NOT propagate __proto__ to Object.prototype — it treats the key as a plain string property on the parsed result object, not as a prototype setter. Empirical verification on Node.js v22.22.2 confirms: after JSON.parse('{"__proto__":{"polluted":true}}'), ({}).polluted is undefined. The constructor.prototype vector is similarly inert through JSON.parse. Prototype pollution via JSON.parse only occurs when the parsed result is subsequently merged into an existing object using an unsafe merge function (e.g. lodash.merge, deepmerge). In node-rsa, $getDecryptedData merely returns the parsed result to the caller — it never merges it into any existing object within the library. The code path is real (line 3883 exists, the 'json' encoding option is documented in README line 251, and it is reachable via the public decrypt/decryptPublic API with no auth required), but the vulnerability mechanism (prototype pollution) does not occur. The finding conflates 'JSON.parse on untrusted input' with 'prototype pollution', which requires an additional unsafe merge step that is absent here.
